success_surface: Robust handling of sparse tabular data with variable participation patterns through programmatic row iteration and filtered value collection
effective_behaviors: Collect all valid numeric values from a target range before performing calculations, filtering out None/NaN/blank entries
Use programmatic row iteration for multi-row cell operations instead of hardcoding individual cell addresses
Handle edge cases where data count falls below expected thresholds (e.g., fewer than 4 scores)
Preserve workbook integrity by saving modified versions to new file paths
Add validation logging to confirm correct execution after computation
Use native Excel data types and respect existing sheet structure
evidence: Collected scores from columns B-P using loop with type checking (isinstance(value, (int, float))) and pd.isna() filter
Iterated over rows 2-5 with range(2, 6) instead of processing each cell individually
Applied conditional logic: if len(scores) >= 4 use last_four_scores = scores[-4:] else use all available
Saved output to '/mnt/data/outputs/...' path rather than modifying source file in-place
Printed calculated averages for each row (Q2:Q5) to validate final values match expectations
Maintained existing worksheet structure while only populating target column Q cells

memory_items.title: Sparse Data Collection Pattern
memory_items.lesson: When dealing with tabular data containing gaps or blanks, iterate through the relevant range and collect only valid numeric values into a list before performing any aggregation or transformation
memory_items.generalization: Filter None/NaN/empty values during the collection phase, not during calculation, to maintain clean data pipelines and enable reliable subsequent operations like slicing or averaging

memory_items.title: Programmatic Row Iteration
memory_items.lesson: For cell-level manipulations affecting multiple rows, use a single loop over row indices rather than separate operations per cell to reduce redundancy and ensure consistency
memory_items.generalization: Define the target row range once, then apply the same extraction-calculation-writing pattern within the loop for uniform results across all affected rows

memory_items.title: Threshold-Aware Calculations
memory_items.lesson: When computing rolling windows (e.g., last N values), include fallback logic that uses all available data when the threshold cannot be met
memory_items.generalization: Check data availability before applying window constraints; gracefully degrade to partial data rather than returning errors or empty results

memory_items.title: Workbook Preservation Protocol
memory_items.lesson: Always write modified spreadsheets to a new output file path rather than overwriting the source, unless explicitly instructed otherwise
memory_items.generalization: This prevents accidental data loss and allows for easy rollback verification; use consistent naming conventions for source vs. output files

edits.content:
## 6. Sparse & Variable-Length Data Handling
- **Collect Before Process**: When working with rows/columns containing gaps (blank cells, NaN values), first iterate through the target range and collect only valid numeric values into a list. Filter None, NaN, and non-numeric types during collection.
- **Threshold Fallback**: For rolling-window calculations (e.g., "last N" values), check if collected data meets the threshold. If fewer than N items exist, use all available data rather than failing or returning empty.
- **Row-Wise Loops**: Apply the same extract-calculate-write pattern within a single row iteration loop for multi-row operations, avoiding redundant code for each individual cell.
- **Validation Output**: Log computed values per target row to verify correctness before considering the task complete.
